title: Firstrade Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026)
meta_title: Firstrade Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026) | TradingToolsHub
meta_description: Firstrade Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026). Firstrade (4.0★, Free). Step-by-step setup, features walkthrough, and pro tips. Updated 2026.
excerpt: Complete guide to setting up and using Firstrade — from account creation to pro-level tips.
content:
Firstrade is a commission-free US broker founded in 1985 and headquartered in Flushing, New York. It offers zero-cost stock, ETF, mutual fund, and options trades with no per-contract fees—a rare offering among US brokers. With a 4.0/5 rating and a completely free tier that never expires, Firstrade is built for cost-conscious traders and investors who prioritize eliminating trading fees over flashy interfaces. Traders use Firstrade primarily for its zero per-contract options pricing, bilingual English-Chinese support, and wide selection of retirement account types. If you trade frequently or hold options positions, Firstrade's fee structure can save you hundreds or thousands annually.

What is Firstrade?

Firstrade is a commission-free US broker that has operated for over 35 years. It specializes in eliminating trading costs entirely—no commissions on stocks, ETFs, mutual funds, or options, and no per-contract fees on options trades. This stands out significantly because most brokers charge $0.65 to $1.00 per options contract, even major competitors like Interactive Brokers and TD Ameritrade. Firstrade serves options traders, buy-and-hold investors, retirement account holders, and traders who prefer bilingual support. The broker offers access to US stocks, options, ETFs, mutual funds, and fixed-income securities, with core tools like alerts, mobile apps, performance analytics, news feeds, and educational resources.

How to Create Your Firstrade Account

Creating a Firstrade account takes approximately 10-15 minutes and requires basic personal information. Here's the step-by-step process:

  • Go to the Firstrade website and click "Open Account" or "New Account" at the top.
  • Choose your account type: Taxable brokerage, Traditional IRA, Roth IRA, SEP IRA, SIMPLE IRA, or business accounts. Firstrade offers multiple IRA varieties, which is a strong advantage for retirement-focused traders.
  • Enter basic personal information: Full name, date of birth, email, phone number, and physical address. Firstrade will verify your identity against public records.
  • Confirm your employment status and investment experience. Firstrade asks for your occupation, employment status, and annual income to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements.
  • Agree to terms and sign electronically. Review the customer agreement and sign with your digital signature or password authentication.
  • Fund your account. Link a bank account for ACH transfers (1-3 business days), wire money (same or next business day), or mail a check. No minimum deposit is required—you can open and fund with as little as $1.
  • Verify your identity if requested. Firstrade may request additional documents or photo ID verification depending on your application. This typically takes 1-2 business days.
  • Access your account. Once approved, you'll receive login credentials and can begin trading immediately.

The entire process is straightforward and does not require speaking to a human unless you have questions or face identity verification issues.

Setting Up Firstrade for the First Time

Once your account is funded and approved, configure these essential elements to optimize your trading experience:

  • Customize your dashboard: Firstrade's dashboard shows your account summary, positions, buying power, and net worth. Rearrange widgets to prioritize the information you check first—most traders place a watchlist and positions summary front and center.
  • Import or build a watchlist: Add stocks and ETFs you're monitoring. You can create multiple watchlists (e.g., "Daily Scanners," "Retirement Portfolio," "Options Ideas") to organize your research.
  • Enable price alerts: Set alerts for specific stocks at price levels you care about. Alerts notify you via email or mobile push notification when a security crosses your threshold.
  • Review your account permissions: Decide whether to enable options trading and at what level (covered calls only, spreads, or naked options). Firstrade requires you to apply for options approval; this process takes 1-2 business days and is based on your trading experience.
  • Set up two-factor authentication: Use your email or phone number to secure your account with a second verification layer.
  • Download the mobile app: Firstrade offers iOS and Android apps for trading on the go. Install it and log in to ensure you can trade outside of web hours.
  • Verify your market data feed: Firstrade provides real-time stock data and 15-minute delayed options data on the free tier. You can upgrade to real-time options data for additional cost if you trade options actively.

Essential Features You Should Know

1. Commission-Free Trading

The core feature that attracts traders to Firstrade is zero commissions on all stock, ETF, mutual fund, and options trades. No per-contract fees on options means a 10-contract iron condor costs $0 to execute, not $10. This directly impacts profitability, especially for high-frequency options traders or those managing many small positions.

2. Mobile App

Firstrade's iOS and Android apps let you trade, check positions, and monitor alerts from anywhere. The mobile interface is simpler than the web platform but sufficient for checking quotes, placing market and limit orders, and monitoring open positions.

3. Performance Analytics

Firstrade tracks your portfolio performance and generates basic analytics including returns by position, daily/monthly/yearly profit-and-loss, and asset allocation breakdowns. This is useful for tax planning and understanding which trades generated your returns, though it's less detailed than Interactive Brokers' tools.

4. News Feed and Market Data

A built-in news feed aggregates market news, earnings releases, and economic calendars. You can filter by sector or symbol to stay informed on positions you hold. Real-time data is included for stocks; options data is 15-minute delayed on the free tier.

5. Alerts and Notifications

Create price alerts, news alerts, and portfolio alerts (e.g., when your account balance hits a specific level). Notifications arrive via email or mobile push, allowing you to react quickly without constantly monitoring your screen.

6. Education and Research

Firstrade provides educational articles, webinars, and video tutorials covering trading basics, options strategies, and tax-efficient investing. The education section is useful for beginners; advanced traders often supplement with external research.

7. Bilingual Support

Firstrade offers customer support, educational content, and platform interfaces in English and Mandarin Chinese, a significant advantage for Chinese-speaking traders.

Firstrade Pricing: Which Plan Should You Choose?

Firstrade offers a single, completely free account tier with no monthly or annual fees, no minimum deposit, and no subscription upsells:

  • Standard Account: $0/month — Includes commission-free trading, $0 per-contract options fees, real-time stock data, 15-minute delayed options quotes, mobile app access, research tools, and customer support. This tier never expires and never requires a minimum balance.

Firstrade's pricing simplicity is its biggest advantage. You never graduate from "free tier" to "paid tier" based on activity—all accounts remain free. If you want real-time options data (useful for active options traders), there is a nominal upgrade cost, but the base platform remains cost-free.

Which plan for which trader?

  • Beginners and buy-and-hold investors: The Standard account is perfect. You get commission-free stock and ETF trading with no minimum deposit, no required activity, and no fees. Costs literally zero forever.
  • Options traders: The Standard account is ideal, especially if you trade options frequently. $0 per-contract fees on spreads, straddles, and other multi-leg strategies save money fast. If you need real-time options quotes, upgrade the data feed.
  • Active traders: Firstrade works well if your primary strategy involves stocks, ETFs, or options. If you also need futures, forex, or API access, Firstrade lacks these and you should compare with Interactive Brokers or TD Ameritrade.

Pro Tips for Getting the Most Out of Firstrade

1. Master the watchlist to screen your ideas. Use multiple watchlists to organize potential trades by strategy. Tag "Morning Scans" with candidates you've identified, then cross-reference them with technical levels and earnings dates before deciding to trade.

2. Use price alerts instead of monitoring charts all day. Set alerts $0.50-$1.00 away from key support and resistance levels on stocks you follow. This frees you to focus on analysis instead of staring at quotes waiting for a move.

3. Leverage zero per-contract fees for multi-leg options strategies. A 10-contract iron condor that would cost $10 on traditional brokers costs $0 on Firstrade. This is where Firstrade shines—use it to trade spreads, straddles, and other strategies that benefit from near-zero execution costs.

4. Plan for options approval delays. Firstrade's options approval process takes 1-2 business days. Apply for the options trading level you need (Levels 1-4) before you plan to trade options—don't wait until you spot a trade.

5. Check the performance analytics monthly to identify your best and worst trades. Review which positions generated your profits and which caused losses. This feedback loop improves decision-making over time.

6. Use the education section to understand the platform, especially if you're new to options. Firstrade's content covers basic strategies and risk management; use it to shore up gaps in your knowledge before risking capital.

7. Combine Firstrade with external data if you need advanced charting or analysis. Firstrade's charting tools are basic compared to TD Ameritrade's thinkorswim. If you need advanced technical analysis, consider using external platforms (TradingView, for example) for analysis while executing on Firstrade to save commissions.

Common Firstrade Issues and How to Fix Them

Issue 1: Account Approval Takes Longer Than Expected

If your account hasn't been approved within 1-2 business days, contact Firstrade support via chat or phone. Delays usually occur due to address verification failures or incomplete employment information. Follow up with support and re-submit any missing documents immediately.

Issue 2: Options Approval is Restricted to Level 1 When You Need Level 3 (Spreads)

Firstrade automatically assigns an options approval level based on your stated experience and account age. If you're approved for Level 1 (calls and puts) but want to trade spreads (Level 3), reapply with evidence of prior options trading experience (brokerage statements from other accounts, for example) or wait 30-60 days and reapply. Firstrade sometimes upgrades approvals automatically with time and account history.

Issue 3: Mobile App Crashes or Fails to Load Quotes

Force-close the Firstrade mobile app, clear the app cache (Settings → Apps → Firstrade → Clear Cache), and restart your phone. Log back in. If the issue persists, check your internet connection and try the web platform. Restart your phone or reinstall the app if the problem continues.

Issue 4: Delayed or Missing Deposits

ACH transfers typically take 1-3 business days. Wire transfers arrive same-day or next business day. If your transfer hasn't arrived after the expected window, contact Firstrade support with your transaction confirmation number. Provide your bank's reference number and Firstrade's account number to investigate delays.

Is Firstrade Worth It? Our Verdict

Firstrade is worth using if you prioritize eliminating trading costs. At 4.0/5 and completely free forever with zero commissions and zero per-contract options fees, Firstrade delivers exceptional value for stock investors, ETF buyers, and especially options traders who execute multi-leg strategies frequently. The platform's basic charting and lack of futures/forex/API access are genuine limitations; however, these only matter if you need those specific features. Compare Firstrade against Interactive Brokers (if you need API access or trading on margin) and TD Ameritrade's thinkorswim (if you prioritize advanced charting), but for pure cost elimination combined with usable core features, Firstrade is a smart choice. Best for options traders seeking zero per-contract fees, cost-conscious buy-and-hold investors, and bilingual traders. Look elsewhere only if you need futures, forex, or algorithmic trading capabilities.
author: TradingToolsHub Editorial
